name: Polish-Lithuanian trunk: Lietuvos Didžioji Kunigaikštystė → Rzeczpospolita Obojga Narodow → Rzeczpospolita Polska
description: The Polish-Lithuanian state: the Grand Duchy of Lithuania (1236+) united with the Polish Crown through the Union of Lublin (1569) to form the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (Rzeczpospolita Obojga Narodów), Europe's largest state by the 16th century. Partitioned out of existence (1772, 1793, 1795) by Russia, Prussia, Austria; restored 1918 as the Second Polish Republic.
ethical_notes: The Commonwealth's 'Golden Liberty' democracy was noble-only; peasants were serfs. The 'szlachta' noble class (10% of population) voted the king, but the liberum veto paralyzed decision-making. The three Partitions (1772-1795) erased Polish sovereignty for 123 years — a national trauma. Post-1945 Poland was moved 200km west (Yalta/Potsdam) with massive population transfers of Germans eastward and Poles westward.

sources.citation: Davies, N. 'God's Playground: A History of Poland' (1981, 2 vols)

links.description: Grand Duchy of Lithuania, founded c. 1236 by Mindaugas. Last pagan state in Europe (Christianized 1387). Extended from Baltic to Black Sea under Vytautas (r. 1392-1430).

Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, established by the Union of Lublin (1 July 1569). A single elected king, shared parliament, but distinct legal systems. The Commonwealth peaked c. 1600 as the largest state in Europe (~1.2 million km²). Partitioned away 1772-1795.

Second Polish Republic, proclaimed 11 November 1918 after WWI. Fought the Soviet-Polish War (1919-1921) to determine its eastern borders. Partitioned again 1939 (Molotov-Ribbentrot) between Nazi Germany and USSR. Polish People's Republic 1947-1989, then democratic Third Republic.
links.ethical_notes: Poland under Nazi occupation saw the Holocaust with half the Jewish population of the country (~3 million) murdered. Warsaw Ghetto Uprising (1943), Warsaw Uprising (1944) — both crushed. The post-1945 communist period (Katyń massacre 1940, post-war Stalinism, Solidarity movement 1980-89) shaped contemporary Polish identity.

name: Mesoamerican Classic-to-Colonial trunk: Teotihuacan → Tōllān → Ēxcān Tlahtōlōyān → Nueva España → Primer Imperio Mexicano
description: Central Mexican state succession: from Teotihuacan (the great Classic-era metropolis, -200 to 550 CE) through the Toltecs of Tula (674-1122), the Aztec Triple Alliance (1428-1521), Spanish colonial Nueva España (1521-1821), to independent Mexico (1821). This trunk represents the political center of gravity in central Mexico over 2200+ years.
ethical_notes: The 1521 Spanish conquest marked a demographic catastrophe — smallpox, war, and enslavement reduced the indigenous population by 80-90% within a century. Nueva España's 300 years of colonial rule created caste-based society (peninsulares > criollos > mestizos > indigenas) whose structures persist. Mexican independence (1821) retained criollo dominance; indigenous rebellions (Tzotzil 1712, Yaqui late 19th c., Chiapas 1994) challenge the national-unity narrative.

sources.citation: Meyer, M. C. & Sherman, W. L. 'The Course of Mexican History' (2002)

links.description: Teotihuacan ('the place where gods were born'), the great Classic-era Mesoamerican metropolis (c. 200 BCE - 550 CE). Population peaked ~125,000, making it among the largest cities in the world. Cultural influence reached Maya lowlands. Declined after internal burning of the ceremonial center (~550 CE).

Tōllān (Tula), the Toltec capital, rising after Teotihuacan's decline. Dominated central Mexico 900-1122 CE. Quetzalcoatl legends linked to Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl (c. 980 CE). Destroyed by political collapse / Chichimec pressure c. 1122.

Mexica (Aztec) Triple Alliance (Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, Tlacopan), formed 1428 after defeating Azcapotzalco. Dominated central Mexico for ~93 years. Capital Tenochtitlan was the largest city in the Americas at Spanish contact.

Viceroyalty of New Spain, established after Cortés destroyed Tenochtitlan (13 August 1521). Covered Mexico, Central America, Caribbean, Philippines, and western US. Extractive colonial economy (silver mines of Potosí/Zacatecas), castas society, missionization.
links.ethical_notes: The conquest involved massive violence, enslavement, disease-driven demographic collapse (90% mortality), and cultural destruction (burning of codices). This was genocide by 20th-century UN definition.

links.description: First Mexican Empire (1821-1823), short-lived monarchy under Agustín de Iturbide after independence from Spain (Treaty of Córdoba, 24 August 1821). Replaced by the First Mexican Republic 1824.
